nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; tends to occur along outer reef faces and drop-offs, generally at depths of 3–60 m (most commonly 10–20 m). Congregates in mid-water above coral formations.

diet.notes: Primarily planktivorous in the wild, with documented consumption of copepods, tunicates, small crustaceans, and fish eggs. In captivity tends to accept a variety of meaty preparations (mysis, brine shrimp, finely chopped seafood), herbivore flakes, and pellets. Multiple small feedings daily are generally advised to mimic natural grazing behavior. Vitamin-enriched foods have been reported to help maintain vibrant coloration.

summary: A vivid, electric-blue schooling damselfish from the Caribbean that is generally considered one of the more beginner-friendly saltwater fish. Tends to be peaceful toward non-conspecifics and is widely regarded as reef-safe.
careNotes:
The Blue Reef Chromis (Chromis cyanea) is native to the Western Atlantic, ranging from Bermuda and southern Florida through the Caribbean Sea, Bahamas, Gulf of Mexico, and Antilles. In the wild it typically inhabits outer reef faces at 10–20 m depth, congregating in mid-water aggregations above coral structure.

Care level disagreement: LiveAquaria and Top Shelf Aquatics rate this species 'easy/beginner'; reefapp.net notes average hardiness and some sensitivity during transport. The consensus leans beginner, though juveniles and newly imported specimens can be delicate.

Minimum tank size disagreement (needs_review): LiveAquaria and Top Shelf Aquatics recommend 30 gallons (≈114 L) as a minimum, while reefapp.net lists 300 L (79 gallons) for a group. These figures differ by roughly 2.6×. The averaged value of ≈207 L is used here; a reviewer should assess whether 114 L or 300 L better reflects a responsible minimum for a small group.

Adult size: Trade sources commonly cite 5 inches (12.7 cm) while FishBase records a maximum of 15.0 cm TL. Animal Diversity Web records an average of 13 cm with a max of 15 cm. A range of 10–15 cm is used here.

Temperature: LiveAquaria gives 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C); FishBase preferred range is 25.6–28.1°C; Animal Diversity Web notes 21–27°C. Averaged to approximately 23–27°C.

Conspecific dynamics: Adult males have been documented as highly territorial toward conspecifics, defending territories of 3–20 m². Groups of juveniles generally school more cohesively; in smaller aquariums dominance hierarchies can emerge, with larger individuals harassing smaller ones. Keeping groups of six or more in sufficiently large tanks has been reported to spread aggression more broadly. Keeping a single specimen or a very small group in an undersized tank may lead to chronic stress for subordinate individuals.

Reproduction: Oviparous with monthly lunar-synchronized spawning cycles. Males are the sole parental caregivers, guarding and aerating adhesive benthic eggs. Breeding in captivity has been reported but is considered difficult owing to the specialized conditions needed. Males perform elaborate signal jumps and zig-zag courtship displays.

Not protogynous: Unlike many other reef fish, Chromis cyanea appears to be gonochoristic (fixed sex) rather than a sequential hermaphrodite. No protogynous sex change has been documented for this species.

Note on scientific name: FishBase uses the synonym Azurina cyanea in some records, but Chromis cyanea (Poey, 1860) is the currently accepted name under Eschmeyer's Catalog of Fishes.

RESOLVED 2026-06-03: Two issues addressed. (1) Minimum tank size: The averaged 207 L figure was not a valid nice-value and sat between conflicting sources. LiveAquaria lists 30 gallons (~114 L) for a single specimen; reefapp.net lists 300 L for a medium specimen kept long-term. Because this is a schooling species requiring a group of 3+, and reefapp.net's 300 L is already for one medium animal, 300 L is adopted as the responsible group minimum. minVolumeLiters updated from 207 to 300. (2) Sex change: fish.sexChange was incorrectly set to "protogynous". The careNotes already stated correctly that Chromis cyanea is gonochoristic (fixed sexes, no sex change). Animal Diversity Web explicitly classifies the species as "gonochoric/gonochoristic/dioecious." Per schema, sexChange="none" denotes gonochoristic; corrected from "protogynous" to "none".
breedingNotes: Oviparous and likely to spawn on a monthly lunar cycle in well-maintained reef aquariums, though successful rearing of larvae in captivity has been reported rarely. Males establish and defend territories, performing signal-jump displays to attract females. Eggs are adhesive and deposited on hard substrate; the male guards and aerates them until hatching. Larvae are pelagic and extremely small, making captive rearing challenging. Breeding difficulty is rated difficult.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (15 cm TL max), temperature range (25.6–28.1°C preferred), native range (Western Atlantic/Caribbean), diet (zooplankton/copepods), and taxonomy. FishBase uses the synonym Azurina cyanea in some records.

additional.name: Animal Diversity Web
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (avg 13 cm, max 15 cm), temperature (21–27°C), lifespan (exceeds 5 years captive), diet composition (52% copepods, 34% tunicates), and reproductive biology (lunar spawning, male parental care, gonochoristic).

additional.name: Top Shelf Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked care level (easy), temperament (peaceful), temperature (72–78°F), pH (8.1–8.4), SG (1.020–1.025), minimum tank (30 gal). Agrees with LiveAquaria on most parameters; recommends groups of 6+ in larger tanks.

additional.name: reefapp.net
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (15 cm), minimum tank (300 L / 79 gal — notably higher than LiveAquaria's 30 gal; tank size disagreement documented in careNotes), grouping notes (1 male to 5+ females), and acclimation sensitivity.

fish.breedingNotes: Oviparous; lunar-synchronized monthly spawning. Males guard and aerate adhesive benthic eggs. Captive rearing of larvae is rarely successful due to extremely small larval size and specialized feeding requirements.
